topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The blue light of the phone screen was the only illumination in Leo’s room, casting harsh shadows across the textbooks piled on his desk. He scrolled mindlessly, the familiar ache settling deep in his chest with every notification. Then, a familiar profile picture popped up: Ms. Albright. His history teacher. Leo froze, his thumb hovering over the screen. It was her. Not just a public profile, but a private one, showing posts about weekend hikes and obscure poetry readings. A cold knot tightened in his stomach, pulling the air from his lungs. He felt a strange, heavy paralysis. He reread the caption under a recent photo—a sunset over a distant mountain range. It was too intimate. A wave of nausea washed over him, and he quickly minimized the app, shoving the phone under his pillow as if hiding a dangerous secret. The silence of the room suddenly felt suffocating.

[story 2]

Maya stared blankly at the cracked ceiling of the library study carrel. The fluorescent lights hummed with an irritating, relentless buzz, mirroring the static in her own mind. She hadn't eaten anything substantial all day, just managed to keep her eyes open long enough to notice the strange activity on her feed. There, in the stream of updates, was Professor Evans. He was posting photos of his garden, meticulous close-ups of rare succulents. The sheer normalcy of it was jarring, an unwelcome intrusion into the gray landscape of her existence. A tremor ran through her hands, making the pencil she clutched feel slick. She felt a profound sense of being utterly unseen, yet simultaneously intensely observed. She closed her eyes, pressing the heels of her palms against her temples, trying to physically push the images away, but they clung to the edges of her vision like stubborn dust.

[story 3]

It was late, the kind of late where the world outside the window seemed muted and distant. Sam was tracing patterns on the condensation of his water bottle, the rhythmic tapping a desperate attempt to anchor himself to something tangible. He’d been scrolling through his feed, seeking any distraction from the dull, pervasive emptiness that had taken root over the last few weeks. Then, there it was—a post from Mr. Harrison. A candid shot of him laughing with a group of colleagues, the light catching the crinkles around his eyes. Sam’s breath hitched. A strange, hollow sensation spread through his limbs, making him want to curl into a tight ball. He felt exposed, as if the digital gaze had somehow pierced the thin veneer of his solitude. He didn't move, just watched the screen, the silence amplifying the frantic, internal churning of his anxiety.
